title: 𒋳𒈠 𒀀𒉿𒈝
titleEn: Laws of Hammurabi — §1–10: justice and theft (v 26 – vii 61)
part: Laws §1–10 · justice and theft (Col. v 26 – vii 61)
draftNote: The § chips number the laws (Harper’s sectioning, still standard); long laws span several tap-units. Readings checked sign-by-sign against Harper’s 1904 edition.
source: Louvre stele, after R.F. Harper, The Code of Hammurabi, King of Babylon (Chicago, 1904): transliteration pp. 10–15, autograph Plates VII–XI, List of Signs. Readings are Harper’s in modern subscript notation. Cuneiform in Old Babylonian monumental sign forms via the SantakkuM webfont by Sylvie Vanséveren (CC BY 4.0); pronunciation: conventional reconstructed Old Babylonian. Fresh translations made directly from the Akkadian; Harper’s public-domain translation consulted for sense only.
about: “At that time:” — and the law begins. §1–5 guard the court itself: false accusers of murder and sorcery (settled by the divine River), lying witnesses, and the judge who alters his own sealed verdict. §6–10 turn to theft: temple and palace goods, the paperless purchase, the sliding scale of thirtyfold, tenfold, or death — and the great stolen-goods trial of §9–10, where both sides bring their witnesses and the seller hangs by the evidence. Legal prose, formulaic on purpose: šumma “if” opens every law, iddâk “he shall be killed” closes too many of them.

sentences.n: The very first law of the stele: false accusation of murder costs the accuser his own life. šumma “if” opens every law — you will read it three hundred times before the stele is done. iddâk “he shall be killed” (from dâkum) is the code’s most famous verb; the -ma on ubbirma chains the clauses of the protasis, exactly as it chained epithets in the prologue.

sentences.n: kišpū is black magic — an accusation impossible to prove by witnesses. So the case goes where no human court can: to the divine River.

sentences.n: The river ordeal. The River is written {d}ÍD — the river-sign wearing the god-marker — because here the Euphrates itself is the judge. The accused leaps in; the god decides who surfaces.

sentences.n: Drowning is the verdict: iktašassu, “it has overcome him” — the same kašādum that meant “attain” in the prologue, now grim. The winner of the case walks off with the loser’s house.

sentences.n: ūtebbibaššuma — “it has made him clean” — is the prologue’s purifying verb ebēbum, now spoken by the water itself. The false accuser inherits the death he aimed at another.

sentences.n: šībūt sarrātim, “testimony of lies.” Perjury, like false accusation, is measured against what it would have cost the victim.

sentences.n: dīn napištim — a case where a life hangs on the verdict. Lie in one, and the life you gambled is your own.

sentences.n: The first Sumerograms of the laws: ŠE, the barley-sign, and KÙ.BABBAR, “shining silver” — the code’s money. Perjury in a property case costs you exactly what the loser would have paid.

sentences.n: dajjānum, the judge, spelled out da-a-a-nu-um. The three verbs march in order: he judged, he decided, he sealed. The sealed tablet (kunukkum) is what makes the reversal provable — the Babylonians could check the paperwork.

sentences.n: adi 12-šu, “up to its twelvefold” — the first of the code’s multiplied damages, and the 12 stands on the stone as a numeral: the ten-sign and two wedges. Watch the pattern: 12 for a judge, 30 for temple theft, 10 for a commoner’s loss.

sentences.n: A public unseating: the chair (kussûm, wearing the wooden-thing marker {giš}) is the office. Harper notes the carver’s slip in the last word — the stone writes uš-ta-ab where uš-ša-ab was meant. Even here, a typo.

sentences.n: makkūrum “property” wears its Sumerian dress (Harper reads the signs ŠA.GA; modern editions NÍG.GA). God and palace — temple and crown — are the two great estates of the land, and theft from either is capital.

sentences.n: The receiver dies with the thief — the code’s standing rule for stolen temple and palace goods.

sentences.n: A shop-window of Sumerograms, one per commodity: KÙ.BABBAR silver, KÙ.GI gold, ARAD and GEME₂ the enslaved man and woman, GU₄ the ox, UDU the sheep, ANŠE the donkey. Flip the toggle to sounds and watch them all turn Akkadian. Property in this code includes people — translated as what it says.

sentences.n: The crime is the paperless deal: buying from someone who cannot legally sell — a minor, a slave — with no witnesses and no riksātum, no contract. šarrāq iddâk: “he is a thief; he shall be killed” — four signs of verdict.

sentences.n: Ordinary theft now — and two more Sumerograms for the collection: ŠAḪ the pig and MÁ the boat.

sentences.i: if it belonged to a god or the palace, he shall pay thirtyfold; if to a commoner, he shall restore tenfold.

sentences.n: The muškēnum — written MAŠ.EN.KAK — is the free commoner, the code’s middle rank between the awīlum and the slave. The arithmetic of rank: the temple’s ox costs a thief thirty oxen, the commoner’s ten. Both numbers stand as numerals on the stone.

sentences.i: If the thief has nothing to pay with, he shall be killed.

sentences.n: The bottom of the sliding scale: restitution if you can, death if you cannot. Property law with an executioner waiting behind it.

sentences.n: The longest law of the ten: the stolen-goods trial. Everything turns on ḫulqum, “the lost thing,” a word you will now meet a dozen times, and on who can produce witnesses.

sentences.n: Watch the little -mi glued to nādinānum-mi and šībī-mi: it is Akkadian’s quotation mark, flagging reported speech. The stone is quoting the defendant.

sentences.n: Both sides produce their proof: the šayyāmānum (buyer) his seller and purchase-witnesses, the owner his identification-witnesses. Now the court can work.

sentences.n: maḫar ilim, “before the god”: testimony is sworn in the temple, where lying has a different price. The verdict falls on the seller — the man who put stolen goods into honest hands.

sentences.i: The owner takes back his lost property, and the buyer recovers from the seller’s estate the silver he had paid.

sentences.n: Nobody honest loses: the owner gets his goods, the duped buyer gets his silver back from the dead seller’s house. išqulu, “he weighed out,” is literal — silver was weighed, not counted.

sentences.n: The mirror-image of §9: no seller, no witnesses, no story. §11–13, which open the next installment, complete this little procedure — the false claimant, the dead seller, the witnesses who need time.

glossary.šumma: if — the word that opens every law of the code
glossary.awīlum: man; the free citizen, the code’s first legal class
glossary.abārum: to accuse; D-stem ubburum: ubbirma “he accused”, mubbirum “accuser”
glossary.-mi: the quotation-particle — marks words as quoted speech
glossary.-ma: and (clause-chaining particle); also “it is X that…”
glossary.nadûm: to cast, throw (a charge; a body into the river): iddi, nadû, iddû
glossary.kânum-D: D-stem of kânum: to convict, prove against someone (uktīn, ukannūšuma)
glossary.dâkum: to kill; N-stem iddâk “he shall be killed” — the code’s death sentence
glossary.lū: or; either (lū … lū “whether … or”); with verb: “let …”
glossary.Nārum: the divine River ({d}ÍD) — judge by ordeal; the accused leaps in and the god decides
glossary.kašādum: to reach, overcome: iktašassu “(the River) has overcome him”
glossary.tabālum: to take away, carry off: itabbal
glossary.ebēbum: to be clean; D-stem: ūtebbibaššuma “(the River) has cleared him”
glossary.šalāmum: to be safe, whole: ištalmam “he came out safe”
glossary.dīnum: case, lawsuit; judgment: dīn napištim “a case of life”
glossary.waṣûm: to go out; ūṣiamma “he came forward”
glossary.qabûm: to speak, say: iqbû, iqtabi, iqabbûma
glossary.kaspum: silver — the code’s money, written KÙ.BABBAR “shining precious-metal”
glossary.našûm: to bear, carry: ittanašši “he shall bear (the penalty)”
glossary.diānum: to judge: dīnam idīn “he judged a case”
glossary.ezēbum: to leave; Š-stem šūzubum: ušēzib “he deposited (a document)”
glossary.enûm: to change, revoke: īteni “he altered”, enêm “altering”
glossary.nadānum: to give, pay: inaddin, iddinam, iddinušum; nadānim “to give”
glossary.kussûm: chair, seat ({giš}GU.ZA) — the seat of office
glossary.tebûm: to rise; Š-stem: ušetbûšuma “they shall make him rise (be removed)”
glossary.târum: to return: ul itârma “he shall never again …”
glossary.wašābum: to sit: ul uššab “he shall not sit” (the stone writes uš-ta-ab — a carver’s slip Harper flags)
glossary.makkūrum: property, assets — written with the Sumerogram Harper reads ŠA.GA (modern NÍG.GA)
glossary.ilum: god; here: the temple as owner
glossary.ekallum: palace (É.GAL “great house”) — the crown as owner
glossary.qātum: hand: ina qāt “from the hand of”
glossary.mārum: son (DUMU) — here: a minor, not yet able to sell
glossary.šâmum-buy: to buy: ištām, išāmu, iššāmu, ašām “I bought” (a homonym of šâmum “to fix destinies”)
glossary.šarrāqum: thief: šarrāq iddâk “he is a thief — he shall be killed”; šarrāqānum “the thief in question”
glossary.muškēnum: commoner, dependent freeman (MAŠ.EN.KAK) — the code’s second rank
glossary.išûm: to have: lā īšu “he has not”
glossary.ḫalāqum: to be lost, vanish: ḫalqu, ḫalqam
glossary.ḫulqum: the lost thing, lost property; ḫuluqšu “his lost property”
glossary.maḫar: before, in the presence of: maḫar ilim “before the god”
glossary.wabālum: to bring, carry: itbalam “he brought”, lū ublam “let me bring”
glossary.amārum: to see, examine: immarūma “they shall examine”
glossary.mūdûm: one who knows: šībī mūdi ḫulqišu “witnesses who know his lost property”; mūdûssunu “what they know”
glossary.šaqālum: to weigh out (silver = to pay): išqulu
glossary.bēlum: owner, lord: bēl ḫulqim “owner of the lost property”
